trend_digest: Trend 1: Mamba/State-Space Models for medical imaging — Hybrid Attention-Mamba architectures (UAM, MoEMambaMIL) show that selective state-space models can capture long-range dependencies with linear complexity, outperforming pure transformers on volumetric data while using far less memory.

Trend 2: Memory-augmented and continual adaptation for segmentation — Papers like 'Shifting Adaptation from Weight Space to Memory Space' and CLoPA demonstrate that lightweight, test-time or continual parameter-efficient adaptation dramatically improves cross-domain generalization without full retraining.

Trend 3: Reinforcement learning and reward-guided fine-tuning for 3D medical generation — 'Optimizing 3D Diffusion Models via Multi-Scale Reward Learning' shows RL-based fine-tuning with clinically-relevant reward signals can bridge the gap between training objectives and downstream task quality.

Trend 4: Multi-modal fusion with task-aware optimization — Fuse4Seg introduces bi-level optimization that jointly optimizes image fusion and segmentation, showing that task-driven fusion outperforms perception-optimized fusion for boundary-sensitive medical segmentation.

Trend 5: Resource-efficient foundation models and cross-modal transfer for radiology — GreenRFM and K-MaT demonstrate that carefully designed pretraining and prompt-learning frameworks can achieve state-of-the-art performance with significantly fewer resources, enabling efficient transfer across imaging modalities.

ideas.title: Hybrid Mamba-Conv Encoder for nnU-Net
ideas.hypothesis: Replacing the deepest encoder stages of nnU-Net with Mamba (selective state-space) blocks will capture long-range spatial dependencies across 3D volumes more efficiently than convolutions alone, improving segmentation of large, dispersed structures (e.g., vasculature, tumors) while reducing GPU memory for high-resolution volumes.
ideas.approach: Modify nnU-Net's encoder architecture: keep the first 2-3 stages as standard residual convolution blocks (for local features), then replace stages 4+ with bidirectional Mamba blocks similar to UAM's unified attention-mamba design. Specifically: (1) In nnunetv2/architecture/encoder.py, add a MambaStage class that uses bidirectional SSM scanning along each spatial axis (similar to Vision Mamba). (2) Modify the PlainConvEncoder to accept a 'mamba_from_stage' config parameter. (3) In the experiment planner, add heuristics for when to activate Mamba stages based on patch size and GPU memory budget. (4) Keep the decoder as transposed-conv + skip connections unchanged. Training: use nnU-Net's existing self-configuring pipeline with no other changes.
ideas.expected_impact: 1-2 Dice points improvement on datasets with large target structures (e.g., liver, kidney tumors in MSD) and 20-40% reduction in peak GPU memory for large patch sizes, enabling higher-resolution training.
relevant_papers: UAM: A Unified Attention-Mamba Backbone of Multimodal Framework for Tumor Cell Classification (2511.17355)
MoEMambaMIL: Structure-Aware Selective State Space Modeling for Whole-Slide Image Analysis (2603.06378)

ideas.title: Test-Time Memory-Augmented Adaptation for nnU-Net
ideas.hypothesis: Augmenting a trained nnU-Net with an external key-value memory bank populated during training (storing encoder features and corresponding label statistics) will allow test-time nearest-neighbor retrieval that corrects predictions on out-of-distribution inputs, improving cross-site generalization without any fine-tuning.
ideas.approach: After standard nnU-Net training: (1) Build a memory bank by running the training set through the frozen encoder and storing (key=bottleneck_feature, value=per-class_dice_weights) pairs. (2) At inference, for each test volume, compute the bottleneck feature, retrieve top-K nearest neighbors from the memory bank, and compute a soft attention-weighted correction signal. (3) Inject this correction as an additive bias to the decoder's deepest skip connection. Implementation: add a MemoryBank class in nnunetv2/inference/ that wraps FAISS for fast retrieval. Modify the predict_from_files pipeline to optionally load and query the memory bank. No retraining needed.
ideas.expected_impact: 2-4 Dice points improvement on cross-site evaluation (e.g., training on site A of AMOS, testing on site B) with negligible inference time overhead (<5% slower due to retrieval).
relevant_papers: Shifting Adaptation from Weight Space to Memory Space: A Memory-Augmented Agent for Medical Image Segmentation (2603.05873)
CLoPA: Continual Low Parameter Adaptation of Interactive Segmentation for Medical Image Annotation (2603.06426)

ideas.title: Task-Aware Multi-Modal Fusion Branch for nnU-Net
ideas.hypothesis: Adding a lightweight fusion branch that combines multiple MRI sequences (or CT phases) using bi-level optimization — where the outer loop optimizes segmentation Dice and the inner loop optimizes per-modality fusion weights — will produce better boundary delineation than nnU-Net's default early concatenation of modalities.
ideas.approach: Modify nnU-Net's multi-modal input handling: (1) Instead of concatenating all modalities at the input, process each modality through a shared-weight shallow encoder (first 2 conv blocks). (2) Add a learnable FusionGate module (small 1x1 conv + sigmoid) that produces per-voxel, per-modality attention weights. (3) Implement bi-level optimization in the trainer: inner loop updates fusion gate parameters on a fusion-quality loss (structural similarity between fused features and ground-truth boundary maps), outer loop updates the full segmentation network on Dice+CE loss. Changes: add FusionGate class in nnunetv2/architecture/, modify nnUNetTrainer to alternate inner/outer optimization steps every N batches.
ideas.expected_impact: 1-3 Dice points improvement on multi-modal datasets (BraTS, AMOS multi-phase CT) especially for boundary-sensitive metrics like HD95, with minimal parameter overhead (<2% extra parameters).
relevant_papers: Fuse4Seg: Image Fusion for Multi-Modal Medical Segmentation via Bi-level Optimization (2409.10328)
K-MaT: Knowledge-Anchored Manifold Transport for Cross-Modal Prompt Learning in Medical Imaging (2603.06340)

ideas.title: RL-Guided Loss Reweighting for nnU-Net Training
ideas.hypothesis: Using a reinforcement learning agent to dynamically reweight the per-class and per-voxel loss during nnU-Net training — with reward based on validation Dice improvement — will handle class imbalance and hard-example mining more effectively than static Dice+CE loss, especially for small structures.
ideas.approach: Add an RL-based loss controller to nnU-Net's training loop: (1) Define a lightweight policy network (2-layer MLP) that takes as input: current per-class Dice scores, epoch number, and per-class loss statistics. (2) The policy outputs per-class loss weights and a hard-example mining threshold. (3) Every K training steps, evaluate on a small validation subset and compute reward = delta_mean_Dice. (4) Update the policy using PPO with the accumulated rewards. Implementation: add RLLossController in nnunetv2/training/loss/, modify nnUNetTrainer.train_step to query the controller for loss weights. The segmentation network itself remains unchanged.
ideas.expected_impact: 2-5 Dice points improvement on small structure segmentation (e.g., pancreas, adrenal glands in MSD/AMOS) where static loss weighting underperforms, with no architectural changes to the base model.
relevant_papers: Optimizing 3D Diffusion Models for Medical Imaging via Multi-Scale Reward Learning (2603.06173)
CORE-Seg: Reasoning-Driven Segmentation for Complex Lesions via Reinforcement Learning (2603.05911)

ideas.title: Efficient Foundation-Model Pretraining for nnU-Net Encoder
ideas.hypothesis: Pretraining nnU-Net's encoder using a resource-efficient self-supervised framework (masked autoencoder with domain-specific augmentations for medical volumes) on a large unlabeled medical image corpus, then plugging the pretrained encoder into nnU-Net's self-configuring pipeline, will improve performance especially in low-data regimes.
ideas.approach: Build a pretraining pipeline for nnU-Net's encoder: (1) Collect unlabeled 3D medical volumes (publicly available CT/MRI from TCIA, UK Biobank subsets). (2) Implement a 3D masked autoencoder (MAE) that operates on the same patch sizes nnU-Net would select. (3) Use GreenRFM's insight: apply anatomy-aware masking (mask ratio adaptive to tissue complexity) rather than random masking. (4) After pretraining, load encoder weights into nnU-Net and proceed with standard nnU-Net training. Implementation: add a pretrain/ module under nnunetv2/ with MAE3D class, a pretraining script, and a weight-loading utility in the experiment planner. The decoder and self-configuration remain untouched.
ideas.expected_impact: 3-5 Dice points improvement in low-data settings (10-30 labeled cases), 1-2 points improvement even with full datasets. Faster convergence (30-50% fewer epochs to reach baseline Dice).
relevant_papers: GreenRFM: Toward a resource-efficient radiology foundation model (2603.06467)
Escaping The Big Data Paradigm in Self-Supervised Representation Learning (2502.18056)
Clinical-Injection Transformer with Domain-Adapted MAE for Lupus Nephritis Prognosis Prediction (2603.05535)
